past_medical_history: High-grade serous ovarian cancer (FIGO stage IC), status post primary cytoreductive surgery including bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, hysterectomy, omentectomy, and pelvic lymph node dissection 18 months ago, currently in remission on surveillance
Hypertension, diagnosed 10 years ago, well-controlled on losartan
Community-acquired pneumonia requiring hospitalization 16 months ago (shortly after cancer surgery)
Migraine without aura, infrequent episodes since age 30, last episode over 2 years ago
Mild depressive episode following cancer diagnosis, treated with sertraline, now discontinued
family_history: Mother died at age 84 from complications of dementia (type not specified)
Father had coronary artery disease, died of myocardial infarction at age 71
Maternal aunt had ovarian cancer diagnosed at age 60
No family history of stroke, epilepsy, or movement disorders

social_history.occupation: Retired accountant, stopped working 6 months ago due to difficulty with numbers
social_history.alcohol: Occasional glass of wine, 1-2 per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband, two adult children live nearby
social_history.exercise: Used to walk 3 miles daily, reduced to occasional short walks over the past year

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person and place; oriented to month but not exact date. MMSE 24/30 (lost 2 points on recall, 2 on serial 7s, 1 on visuospatial copy, 1 on writing). Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) 19/30. Notably impaired on clock drawing (placed numbers incorrectly, could not set hands to correct time). Difficulty with intersecting pentagons. Unable to accurately copy a cube. Spontaneous speech fluent with occasional word-finding pauses. Could not perform serial counting backwards from 100 by 7s past 93.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive. Visual fields full to confrontation. Visual acuity 20/25 bilaterally (corrected). Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation and motor symmetric. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Muscle bulk and tone normal throughout. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. No pronator drift. No tremor at rest or with action.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, and temperature sensation intact throughout. Proprioception intact at great toes bilaterally. Graphesthesia impaired in both hands (could not identify numbers drawn on palms). Stereognosis mildly impaired bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric throughout upper and lower extremities. Plantar reflexes flexor bilaterally. No frontal release signs.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally. Heel-to-shin testing normal. No dysmetria or dysdiadochokinesia.
neurological_exam.gait: Gait steady with normal stride length and arm swing. Romberg negative. Tandem gait intact.
neurological_exam.additional: Mild simultanagnosia noted on complex scene description (missed peripheral details). Difficulty with visual reaching for objects presented in peripheral visual field (optic ataxia). Constructional apraxia evident on clock drawing and cube copy. These findings suggest a posterior cortical syndrome pattern. No environmental agnosia reported by family, but patient recently became lost in a familiar shopping mall.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive difficulty with reading, driving, and spatial orientation over the past 14 months
patient.history_present_illness: Mrs. R is a 62-year-old right-handed woman, a retired accountant, who presents to the neurology memory clinic with a 14-month history of progressive cognitive difficulties. Her symptoms began approximately 4 months after completing adjuvant chemotherapy for ovarian cancer, initially manifesting as subtle difficulty reading financial spreadsheets, which she attributed to post-chemotherapy cognitive impairment ('chemo brain') and stress from her cancer recovery. Over the subsequent months, her husband noticed she was having increasing trouble parallel parking, frequently misjudging distances, and on one occasion she sideswiped a pillar in a parking garage. She stopped driving approximately 6 months ago after becoming disoriented on a familiar route to the grocery store. She reports difficulty reading despite unchanged visual acuity on ophthalmologic examination, describing that 'the words seem to jump around on the page.' She has trouble locating objects on a cluttered table and has begun reaching past items when trying to grasp them. She also reports difficulty judging the depth of stairs and has had two near-falls descending a staircase. Notably, her episodic memory has been relatively preserved — she can recall recent conversations, remembers appointments, and follows the plot of television programs without difficulty, though her husband notes she occasionally repeats a question. She has had increasing difficulty with financial calculations and recently made several errors in balancing the household checkbook, which is out of character given her professional background. She retired from her accounting firm 6 months ago, ostensibly for health reasons related to her cancer history, but her husband privately reports that she was struggling with her work. There has been no personality change, no behavioral disturbance, no hallucinations, no motor symptoms, and no episodes of confusion or fluctuating awareness. She has no headaches, seizures, or focal weakness. Her oncologist confirmed disease remission on recent surveillance imaging. Her prior primary care physician attributed her cognitive symptoms to post-chemotherapy cognitive dysfunction and mild depression, and she was briefly re-started on sertraline, which was discontinued after 3 months without improvement. She was referred to neurology when her symptoms continued to progress.

background.sleep: Not captured during this routine recording

findings.location: Bilateral posterior temporal and parietal regions

artifacts.notes: Intermittent, does not obscure underlying activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: No significant change in background activity
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Physiologic driving response present bilaterally, symmetric

eeg.impression: Normal routine EEG for age. Intermittent posterior temporal-parietal theta activity is a non-specific finding that could represent early cortical dysfunction but could also be within normal limits for age. No epileptiform discharges. No focal slowing to suggest acute structural lesion. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 30-minute recording; prolonged or sleep-deprived EEG may reveal additional findings.

findings.location: Bilateral parietal and occipital lobes, predominantly posterior

signal_characteristics.T1: Widened cortical sulci with prominent posterior sylvian fissures
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: No periventricular signal abnormality beyond mild age-appropriate changes

findings.location: Periventricular and deep subcortical white matter, bilateral

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Hyperintense foci in periventricular caps and scattered in centrum semiovale

signal_characteristics.T1_coronal: Hippocampal volumes at lower end of normal for age, symmetric

volumetrics.hippocampal_volume: Left 2.8 mL, Right 2.9 mL (low-normal for age, symmetric)
volumetrics.parietal_cortical_thickness: Reduced bilaterally, more prominent on right (0.8 SD below age-matched norms)
volumetrics.occipital_cortical_thickness: Reduced bilaterally (0.7 SD below age-matched norms)

additional_observations: No mass lesions or metastatic disease
Mild mucosal thickening in bilateral maxillary sinuses (incidental)
mri.impression: Posterior-predominant cortical atrophy involving bilateral parietal and occipital lobes with relative sparing of medial temporal structures. White matter hyperintensities (Fazekas grade 2) suggest moderate small vessel disease. Hippocampal volumes are low-normal (Scheltens grade 1), not meeting criteria for significant hippocampal atrophy. No acute intracranial pathology. No evidence of metastatic disease. The posterior-predominant atrophy pattern is atypical for age-related changes and raises concern for posterior cortical atrophy or early neurodegenerative process, though prominent white matter disease suggests a possible vascular contribution. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.impression: POSITIVE amyloid PET scan. Diffuse cortical amyloid deposition is present, most prominent in parietal, occipital, and precuneus/posterior cingulate regions. The posterior-predominant distribution correlates with the clinical presentation of posterior cortical atrophy. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.impression: Bilateral parieto-occipital hypometabolism with relative preservation of medial temporal and frontal metabolism. The metabolic pattern correlates well with the clinical presentation of progressive visuospatial dysfunction. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: posterior cortical atrophy Alzheimer's disease diagnosis management

results.title: Posterior cortical atrophy: an atypical variant of Alzheimer disease
results.authors: Crutch SJ, Schott JM, Rabinovici GD, et al.
results.summary: Comprehensive review of PCA as the most common atypical AD variant. Characterized by progressive visuospatial and visuoperceptual dysfunction with relative preservation of memory and language early in the course. Underlying pathology is most commonly AD (>80% of cases), with minority being Lewy body disease or corticobasal degeneration.

results.title: Consensus classification of posterior cortical atrophy
results.authors: Crutch SJ, Lehmann M, Schott JM, et al.
results.summary: Proposed diagnostic criteria for PCA: (1) insidious onset and gradual progression, (2) prominent early disturbance of visual and/or other posterior cognitive functions, (3) core features include space perception deficit, simultanagnosia, object perception deficit, constructional dyspraxia, environmental agnosia, oculomotor apraxia, dressing apraxia, optic ataxia, alexia, and Balint/Gerstmann syndrome features.

results.title: Amyloid and tau biomarkers in posterior cortical atrophy
results.summary: CSF biomarkers and amyloid PET demonstrate that >85% of PCA patients have AD pathology. ATN framework biomarkers show similar profiles to typical AD but with posterior-predominant tau distribution on tau PET. Amyloid PET and CSF Abeta42/tau ratios are recommended for confirming AD as the underlying etiology.

output.summary: Posterior cortical atrophy (PCA) is the most common atypical variant of Alzheimer's disease, accounting for approximately 5% of AD cases. It typically presents in patients aged 50-65 with progressive visuospatial dysfunction, constructional apraxia, and elements of Balint syndrome or Gerstmann syndrome, while episodic memory is relatively preserved early on. Diagnosis is supported by posterior-predominant cortical atrophy on MRI, parieto-occipital hypometabolism on FDG-PET, and positive amyloid biomarkers (PET or CSF). Management follows standard AD treatment guidelines including cholinesterase inhibitors, with additional occupational therapy for visuospatial compensation strategies.

output.proposed: Donepezil 5 mg once daily at bedtime
interactions: Atorvastatin: CYP3A4 substrate overlap; no dose adjustment needed at standard doses
Aspirin: Both may increase GI bleeding risk; monitor for GI symptoms
warnings: May cause bradycardia; monitor heart rate, especially with initiation
GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea, anorexia) common during titration
Caution in patients with cardiac conduction abnormalities
May exacerbate or induce extrapyramidal symptoms in susceptible individuals
output.formulary_status: Preferred formulary agent for mild-moderate Alzheimer's disease
alternatives: Rivastigmine patch 4.6 mg/24hr (if GI intolerance to oral donepezil)
Galantamine 4 mg twice daily (alternative cholinesterase inhibitor)

output.query: chemotherapy-related cognitive impairment chemo brain vs neurodegenerative disease differentiation

results.title: Cancer-related cognitive impairment: an update on state of the art, detection, and management strategies
results.authors: Lange M, Joly F, Vardy J, et al.
results.summary: Chemotherapy-related cognitive impairment (CRCI) typically involves executive function, processing speed, and attention. It usually stabilizes or improves within 1-2 years of treatment completion. Progressive worsening beyond 2 years should raise concern for alternative etiologies including neurodegenerative disease. CRCI rarely involves prominent visuospatial dysfunction.

results.title: Distinguishing neurodegenerative disease from cancer-related cognitive decline
results.authors: Pergolizzi D, Root JC, et al.
results.summary: Key differentiators: CRCI shows predominantly attention/executive dysfunction on neuropsych testing without the progressive amnestic or visuospatial deterioration seen in AD. Neuroimaging in CRCI typically shows diffuse white matter changes without focal cortical atrophy. Progressive visuospatial decline with cortical atrophy on MRI strongly suggests a neurodegenerative process rather than CRCI.

output.summary: Chemotherapy-related cognitive impairment (CRCI/'chemo brain') is common after cancer treatment but typically involves attention, executive function, and processing speed with stabilization within 1-2 years. It does not typically cause progressive visuospatial dysfunction or cortical atrophy. The progressive nature, posterior-predominant cortical atrophy, and visuospatial-predominant neuropsychological profile in this patient are inconsistent with CRCI and strongly suggest an underlying neurodegenerative process.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Early-stage Alzheimer's disease, posterior cortical atrophy (PCA) variant

differential.key_distinguishing: CRCI typically involves attention/executive dysfunction, stabilizes within 1-2 years of treatment, and does not cause progressive visuospatial decline with posterior cortical atrophy. This patient's ongoing progression 18 months post-chemo with posterior-predominant atrophy argues against CRCI.

differential.diagnosis: Cerebral small vessel disease / vascular cognitive impairment
differential.key_distinguishing: Fazekas grade 2 white matter changes and vascular risk factors suggest vascular contribution, but the posterior-predominant cortical atrophy pattern and positive amyloid biomarkers indicate AD as the primary pathology. Vascular disease may be a comorbid accelerating factor.

Can present with visuospatial deficits, but typically accompanied by visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, REM sleep behavior disorder, and cognitive fluctuations — none of which are present. FDG-PET pattern with occipital hypometabolism can overlap, but amyloid-positive CSF and absence of core LBD features make this unlikely.

Can cause posterior cortical dysfunction and apraxia, but typically presents with asymmetric rigidity, alien limb phenomenon, myoclonus, and cortical sensory loss. Motor exam is normal in this patient.

History of mild depressive episode, but sertraline trial did not improve cognition. Neuropsychological profile shows domain-specific visuospatial deficits rather than global effort-dependent poor performance. Objective progressive decline on testing is inconsistent with pseudodementia.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI to assess for structural causes and atrophy pattern
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Posterior-predominant cortical atrophy with relatively preserved hippocampal volumes; white matter changes suggesting vascular disease

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory workup to rule out reversible causes of cognitive decline
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal TSH, B12, folate, RPR, HIV; mildly elevated lipids suggesting vascular risk factors

optimal_actions.action: Request comprehensive neuropsychological testing to characterize cognitive profile
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Prominent visuospatial and visuoconstructive deficits with relatively preserved episodic memory — pattern consistent with posterior cortical atrophy

optimal_actions.action: Order amyloid PET or CSF biomarkers to confirm Alzheimer's disease pathology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive amyloid PET with posterior-predominant uptake; or CSF showing decreased Abeta42, elevated p-tau and t-tau (A+T+N+)

optimal_actions.action: Consider FDG-PET to characterize metabolic pattern and support PCA diagnosis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Parieto-occipital hypometabolism with preserved medial temporal metabolism

optimal_actions.action: Search literature on posterior cortical atrophy to inform diagnosis and management
optimal_actions.expected_finding: PCA is the most common atypical AD variant; diagnosis supported by visuospatial-predominant cognitive profile with positive amyloid biomarkers

optimal_actions.action: Consider APOE genotyping and early-onset AD gene panel given age <65
optimal_actions.expected_finding: APOE e3/e4 (risk factor); no autosomal dominant mutations

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions before initiating cholinesterase inhibitor therapy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Donepezil safe with current medications; monitor for bradycardia and GI effects

critical_actions: Obtain brain MRI to assess atrophy pattern and rule out structural lesions or metastatic disease
Order dementia screening labs (TSH, B12, RPR) to exclude reversible causes
Request neuropsychological testing to characterize the domain-specific cognitive profile
Obtain amyloid biomarkers (PET or CSF) to confirm AD pathology underlying the PCA syndrome
Recognize that the presentation is consistent with posterior cortical atrophy, an atypical AD variant, rather than typical amnestic AD or chemotherapy-related cognitive impairment
contraindicated_actions: Attributing progressive visuospatial decline solely to chemotherapy-related cognitive impairment without further workup
Diagnosing typical amnestic Alzheimer's disease based on MRI hippocampal assessment alone (hippocampi are relatively preserved in PCA)
Prescribing anticholinergic medications which would worsen cognitive function
Initiating empiric antiviral therapy without evidence of infectious encephalitis

Ordering lumbar puncture to evaluate for paraneoplastic/autoimmune encephalitis as the primary diagnosis without first considering the progressive, insidious nature and posterior cortical pattern inconsistent with autoimmune etiology
key_reasoning_points: The progressive visuospatial dysfunction with relatively preserved episodic memory is the hallmark of posterior cortical atrophy, not typical amnestic AD

The history of ovarian cancer and chemotherapy creates a strong red herring — CRCI is a plausible alternative but does not explain progressive posterior cortical atrophy with positive amyloid biomarkers
MRI showing posterior-predominant atrophy with relatively preserved hippocampal volumes (Scheltens grade 1) is atypical for amnestic AD but characteristic of PCA-AD
The prominent white matter changes (Fazekas grade 2) and elevated lipids create another red herring suggesting vascular cognitive impairment as the primary etiology
CSF biomarkers showing A+T+N+ profile and positive amyloid PET confirm AD as the underlying pathology
Normal EEG does not exclude early-stage AD, as EEG changes occur later in the disease course

The broad differential from the source case (perioperative complications, infectious encephalitis, brain metastases) is transformed here into a chronic progressive outpatient presentation where the key diagnostic challenge is distinguishing PCA-AD from chemo brain and vascular cognitive impairment
APOE e4 carrier status supports AD risk but is neither necessary nor sufficient for diagnosis

metadata.condition_name: Early-stage Alzheimer's disease (posterior cortical atrophy variant)
metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle: Patient presents with progressive visuospatial dysfunction initially attributed to chemotherapy-related cognitive impairment ('chemo brain') following ovarian cancer treatment. The posterior cortical atrophy pattern is atypical for classic amnestic AD, and the relatively preserved hippocampal volumes on MRI may mislead toward non-AD diagnoses. Prominent white matter disease adds a vascular red herring. The correct diagnosis requires recognizing PCA as an AD variant and obtaining confirmatory amyloid biomarkers.
